title: Basic white sandwich loaf
subtitle: The everyday tin loaf, made right
excerpt: A proper white sandwich loaf: soft crumb, thin crust, good for toast and sandwiches alike. Mixed in one bowl, no stand mixer needed, baked in a standard 900 g tin.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the basic household bread formula. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the soft sandwich crumb, achieved by scalding a portion of the flour with boiling milk.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice cool, freeze flat in a bag for up to 3 months. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix the night before, let it rise slowly in the fridge overnight, bake in the morning.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; drop to 200°C after 15 minutes to finish the crumb without darkening the crust
baking.steamMethod: water tray on the oven floor for the first 10 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A way to check gluten development: stretch a small piece of dough between your fingers. If it stretches thin enough to be translucent without tearing, the gluten network is sufficiently developed.

content.text: A white sandwich loaf is not a romantic bake. It is a practical one, and a good practical bake done well is worth more than a dramatic one done badly. This loaf is soft inside, thin-crusted, holds together under the weight of butter and keeps for three days without going stale. It is what bread should be when it is bread for everyday.

The recipe uses a 64% hydration, which produces a dough that is just slightly tacky to the hand but easy to shape. The water goes in at blood temperature, around 37°C. Too hot and you slow or kill the yeast; too cold and the first rise will drag.

Combine the {{strong-white-bread-flour}}, {{fine-sea-salt}}, and {{instant-dried-yeast}} in a large bowl, keeping the salt and yeast on opposite sides until the liquid goes in. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} and rub it into the flour with your fingertips until the flour resembles fine crumbs. Pour in the warm {{whole-milk}} and {{water}} together and mix to a shaggy dough.
Turn out onto a clean surface and knead for 10 minutes by hand. The dough should pass the
by the end: stretch a small piece between your fingers and it should be thin enough to be translucent. If it tears immediately at 10 minutes, knead for two minutes more.

Shape the dough into a ball, put it back in the bowl, cover with a damp cloth or cling film, and leave somewhere warm for about 60 minutes. It should roughly double in size. A warm kitchen (20–22°C) takes the full 60 minutes. A cold one takes longer.

Grease the loaf tin. Tip the dough onto a clean surface, press it gently to knock back the gas, then stretch it into a rectangle roughly the width of the tin. Fold the long edges in to the centre and roll it up tightly, seam-side down. Lower it into the tin. It should reach about two-thirds of the way up the sides.

Cover the tin loosely and leave the dough to prove for 45 minutes. It is ready when it has risen about 2–3 cm above the rim of the tin and the dough springs back slowly when you press it lightly with a floured finger. Meanwhile, heat the oven to 220°C fan and place a small roasting tin on the oven floor.

Score the top of the loaf with a sharp knife or razor blade — one slash down the centre, about 1 cm deep. Pour 200 ml of cold water into the roasting tin on the oven floor and slide the loaf onto the shelf above it. Bake for 15 minutes, then drop the temperature to 200°C fan and bake for a further 20 minutes.

The loaf is done when it is deep golden, the sides have pulled slightly from the tin, and it sounds hollow when you tap the base. An instant-read thermometer should read 96°C at the centre. Tip it out of the tin immediately and cool on a rack for at least 30 minutes before slicing. Cutting into a hot loaf compresses the crumb.

After shaping the dough into the tin, cover and refrigerate overnight. In the morning, take it out and let it come to room temperature for about 45 minutes before baking. The slow, cold prove develops more flavour than the quick same-day version.
